reasoning: The US High Yield Spread spiked to 4.61 in early April 2025 following the imposition of new trade tariffs, but has since seen a sharp downward reversion, falling to 3.67 by April 25. The immediate momentum is distinctly downward as markets digest the tariff shock and realize default rates aren't instantly spiking. For the nearest resolution date (May 4), it is highly likely the spread will continue its downward reversion toward pre-tariff levels (low 3s), so probability of an increase is low (28%). For later resolution dates, the probability increases and approaches 50%. This is because the long-term historical average for the OAS is ~4.40%, and potential economic slowdowns from the tariffs or other macro shocks over a 3-6 month horizon could easily push the spread back above 3.67. Therefore, uncertainty increases with time, pushing distant probabilities closer to the base rate (~50%).
